result:
## The Quiet Art of Noticing

There is a kind of wealth that does not announce itself. It does not arrive in the form of applause, gold, promotion, or a room full of people saying your name with admiration. It is quieter than that. It is the wealth of attention: the ability to be present enough to notice the world as it unfolds, not as an obstacle, not as background scenery, but as something inexhaustibly strange and alive.

Most days, we move through life as though we are late for it. We carry our intentions in front of us like lanterns, illuminating only the next task, the next obligation, the next small crisis. The rest of the world falls away. A tree becomes merely shade over a parked car. A stranger becomes an inconvenience in line. Rain becomes traffic. Morning becomes the distance between sleep and work. The ordinary becomes invisible, not because it lacks wonder, but because we have trained ourselves to look through it.

Yet the ordinary is where we live.

No one inhabits the grand moments for very long. Weddings, graduations, funerals, triumphs, accidents, departures—these flare up in memory because they are bright, but they are not the whole fire. Most of life is made of transitional spaces: waiting for water to boil, searching for keys, walking from one room to another and forgetting why, folding laundry, rinsing a cup, standing beneath the shower while the mind rehearses conversations that will probably never happen. If meaning exists only in the exceptional, then most of life is meaningless. But if meaning is something that can be found, made, or received in the ordinary, then every day is dense with possibility.

The trick, if there is one, is to notice.

Noticing is not the same as seeing. Seeing is biological. Noticing is almost moral. It requires a willingness to let the world matter. You can see a sparrow and register only “bird.” You can notice its tiny mechanical hops, the abrupt tilt of its head, the boldness with which it investigates a crumb nearly as large as its beak. You can see an old woman on a bench and register only “person.” You can notice the gloved hand resting on the handle of a cane, the careful arrangement of her coat, the way she turns her face toward the weak winter sun as if accepting a blessing. You can see your own kitchen and think only of dishes. You can notice the small domestic galaxy of crumbs, cups, jars, light, and habit: evidence that you are alive and have been hungry and will be hungry again.

Attention changes nothing, and yet it changes everything.

A city, for example, becomes a different creature depending on the quality of attention brought to it. To the hurried commuter, a city is a system of delays. It is red lights, crowded trains, construction noise, weather at the wrong angle. But to the attentive wanderer, a city is an enormous manuscript written in brick, glass, gum spots, window displays, graffiti, overheard arguments, and the smells escaping from restaurants at dusk. Every block is a paragraph. Every corner has been revised by time.

There is beauty in the way cities refuse to be singular. They are never just one thing. A city is both architecture and appetite. It is ambition and loneliness stacked vertically. It is pigeons strutting like minor officials. It is a delivery driver balancing six impossible bags on a bicycle. It is the tired tenderness of someone carrying flowers through a subway station. It is the opera of garbage trucks before dawn. It is a thousand private griefs walking past a thousand private errands. It is people living side by side without knowing one another, and yet depending on one another in ways too complex to name.

Once, while walking through a neighborhood just after rain, I saw a man in a suit kneel on the sidewalk to help a beetle turn itself upright. The beetle had become stranded on its back, legs moving frantically in the air. The man set down his briefcase, crouched, and used the edge of a receipt to flip the creature over. Then he stood, picked up his briefcase, and continued walking as if nothing unusual had happened. Perhaps nothing unusual had happened. Perhaps small acts of mercy occur constantly and silently, too modest to be recorded. But I remember the gesture because it punctured the usual story of the street, which is that everyone is too busy to care.

A single noticed detail can redeem an hour.

This is not sentimentality. Sentimentality asks the world to be softer than it is. Attention accepts the world as it is, but refuses to reduce it. The attentive person sees the cruelty, the boredom, the waste, the absurdity; but also the beetle, the receipt, the kneeling man. To notice is not to deny suffering. It is to prevent suffering from becoming the only truth.

Children are naturally good at this before they are taught efficiency. A child can spend twenty minutes examining a rock, not because the rock is rare, but because the child has not yet accepted the adult hierarchy of importance. The rock is interesting. Therefore, it deserves attention. Its color changes when wet. Its surface contains flecks like stars. It fits in the palm. It can become treasure, tool, planet, egg, or secret. Adults, by contrast, often need a thing to justify itself before we will look at it. Is it useful? Is it profitable? Is it urgent? Is it content? We forget that interest itself is a form of relationship.

There is a particular sadness in the phrase “just a.” Just a weed. Just a cashier. Just an old song. Just a phase. Just a dream. Just a coincidence. The phrase is often a door closing. It trims mystery down to a manageable size. Of course, life requires categories. We cannot stand astonished before every spoon. We would never get anything done. But perhaps we have leaned too far in the other direction. Perhaps we have become too skilled at dismissal.

To live attentively is to restore some of the world’s depth.

Take weeds. The word itself is less a botanical category than a human complaint. A weed is a plant growing where someone does not want it. But look closely at the crack in a sidewalk where a dandelion has forced its way upward. The plant is not aware of trespassing. It is conducting its ancient business: root, stem, leaf, flower, seed. It is a small yellow argument against concrete. Later, when it becomes a white globe of seeds, a child may lift it and scatter its future with one breath. Is it just a weed? Or is it an emissary from the persistence of life?

The world is full of such emissaries.

There is the smell of bread from a bakery in the early morning, a smell so generous it seems almost public property. There is the sound of someone practicing piano imperfectly through an open window, stopping and starting the same measure until the repetition becomes strangely moving. There is the blue hour after sunset when buildings lose their edges and windows glow as if each room contains a private lantern festival. There is the first sip of water when you wake thirsty in the night. There is the satisfying click of a well-made latch, the papery skin of an onion, the soft thunder of pages when a book is riffled, the tiny rainbow on a wall made by sunlight passing through a glass.

None of these things solves the enormous problems of being human. They do not abolish death, heal all wounds, or explain why people can be so tender and so terrible. But they widen the room in which we face those facts. They give the mind places to rest that are not denial. They say: yes, there is pain, and also the smell of oranges; yes, there is fear, and also a dog asleep with its paws twitching; yes, time is passing, and also listen to the rain beginning again.

Rain may be one of the greatest teachers of attention. It edits the world without erasing it. Streets shine. Leaves darken. Cars become sleek and anonymous. People alter their posture, ducking their heads, sharing awnings, improvising hats from newspapers and tote bags. The sound of rain on a roof can make a room feel more interior, more protected, as though the house has become a ship and the weather an ocean. Rain reveals the surfaces of things: which ground absorbs, which repels, which leaks, which holds. It gives gutters a voice. It paints windows with moving distortions.

Snow, too, changes attention. It simplifies the world visually while complicating it practically. It erases boundaries, softens ugliness, muffles sound. A familiar street after snowfall looks as if it has paused to reconsider itself. Footprints become records of passage, each one a brief inscription: someone came this way; someone went home; someone walked a dog; someone dragged a sled; someone slipped, perhaps cursed, perhaps laughed. Snow makes visible the choreography that is usually lost.

But attention does not belong only to beauty. One can notice decay, neglect, awkwardness, failure. There is dignity in seeing what is damaged without immediately turning away. A boarded-up theater still carries the shape of its former invitation. A broken chair on a curb still remembers the bodies it held. A faded sign on an old shopfront still speaks in the typography of another decade. Even rust has color. Even peeling paint makes patterns. The world is not divided neatly into what is worthy of attention and what is not. Often, the neglected thing becomes luminous under a steady gaze.

This is why artists are so necessary. Not because they decorate existence, though sometimes they do, but because they re-train perception. A painter can make us see that shadows are not gray but blue, violet, green, brown. A poet can return a worn word to its original brightness. A filmmaker can frame a hallway so that waiting becomes suspenseful. A musician can gather noise and reveal its hidden architecture. Art says: look again. Listen again. Feel what you have been moving too quickly to feel.

But one need not be an artist to practice attention. Washing dishes can be an education. The warm water, the slick plate, the transformation from grease to clarity, the small satisfaction of order restored: all of it is available. So is walking. So is eating an apple. So is listening to another person without preparing a reply. Perhaps that is one of the highest forms of attention: listening not as strategy, not as performance, but as hospitality.

To be listened to well is rare enough that we remember it. Most conversations are alternating monologues, negotiations for airtime, or competitions between rehearsed selves. Real listening creates a temporary shelter. It allows another person to hear themselves more clearly. It says, without saying: for this moment, you do not have to compete with the rest of the world. I am here.

Attention is related to love, though they are not identical. You can pay attention without love, as a scientist might study a virus or a detective a crime scene. But love without attention becomes vague, more idea than act. To love someone is, among other things, to notice them: their habits, their silences, the stories they repeat, the foods they avoid, the hour of day when they become most themselves. It is to learn the weather patterns of another soul. Not perfectly—never perfectly—but with care.

Neglect, conversely, is often a failure of attention before it is a failure of feeling. Relationships rarely collapse all at once. They become invisible by degrees. People stop noticing the bid for affection hidden inside a complaint, the fatigue beneath irritability, the courage required for a small confession. They stop noticing the other person as changing and alive. They interact instead with an outdated map. Eventually, the distance between the map and the person becomes too great.

The same can be true of the self. We carry versions of ourselves that may no longer be accurate. We say, “I am not the kind of person who…” or “I always…” or “I could never…” long after the evidence has shifted. To pay attention inwardly is not to become self-absorbed; it is to update the map. What actually gives me energy? What drains me? What am I pretending not to know? What do I keep returning to? What, in my life, has quietly ended? What is trying to begin?

These questions are not always comfortable. Attention can be disruptive. Once you notice the crack in the wall, the tenderness in a voice, the dishonesty in your own explanation, you cannot easily unnotice it. Awareness asks something of us. This may be why distraction is so seductive. It offers relief not only from boredom, but from responsibility. If I do not notice, I do not have to respond.

The modern world has become extremely skilled at harvesting attention while weakening the capacity for it. We are surrounded by devices and systems designed to interrupt us, stimulate us, flatter us, alarm us, and return us endlessly to the surface of things. The problem is not technology itself. A phone can connect, inform, guide, translate, preserve, and delight. The problem is the economy built around capturing the mind in fragments. Attention, once treated as a private faculty, has become a commodity. Something wants it all the time.

This makes deliberate attention a quiet form of resistance. To read a long book, to take a walk without headphones, to watch clouds without photographing them, to cook slowly, to mend something instead of replacing it, to sit with grief without immediately converting it into a post or performance—these are not grand rebellions, but they matter. They reclaim the mind from compulsive scattering.

Of course, no one can live in a state of constant radiant awareness. That would be exhausting, maybe unbearable. We need autopilot. We need routine. We need the mercy of not noticing every sensation, every implication, every passing shadow. The goal is not perpetual intensity. It is availability. It is keeping some part of oneself awake enough to be surprised.

Surprise is one of the great pleasures of attention. The world, when actually encountered, keeps exceeding our summaries of it. The stern neighbor feeds stray cats at midnight. The boring meeting contains one sentence that changes your mind. The town you thought ugly has a street of pear trees in bloom. The person you envied is lonely. The person you dismissed is wise. The day you expected to waste gives you a memory you keep for years.

Memory itself seems to depend less on time than on attention. We forget whole weeks of routine and remember three seconds from childhood: the green smell of a garden hose, the metallic taste of fear before a school performance, dust floating in a shaft of afternoon light. Why these moments? Perhaps because, for whatever reason, we were fully there. Attention pressed the experience into us.

This suggests that a longer life is not only a matter of more years, but of more presence within the years given. A day skimmed is shorter than a day inhabited. A conversation half-heard is less life than a conversation received. If we cannot control the length of our lives, perhaps we can influence their depth.

There is an old tension between doing and being. Much advice urges us to act, build, improve, strive. Much wisdom literature urges us to pause, accept, observe, breathe. Both are necessary. A life of pure action can become frantic and hollow. A life of pure contemplation can become passive or detached. Attention may be the bridge between them. It allows action to arise from contact with reality rather than from reflex. It allows stillness to become engaged rather than inert.

Consider gardening. A gardener cannot merely impose an idea upon the soil. They must observe: where the light falls, where water gathers, which leaves yellow, which insects arrive, what the season permits. Gardening is work, certainly, but it is work guided by attention. The same is true of teaching, parenting, carpentry, medicine, friendship, cooking, and almost any meaningful craft. Skill begins where attention deepens.

Even mistakes become more useful when noticed carefully. A cook who burns garlic learns the speed of heat. A musician who plays a wrong note learns the shape of a phrase. A person who speaks harshly and sees another face close in pain learns the consequence of tone. Without attention, mistakes repeat as if they were fate. With attention, they become instruction.

Perhaps wisdom is accumulated attention: not merely having experiences, but having noticed them.

One can travel the world inattentively and remain provincial. One can live in the same town for fifty years attentively and discover endless countries. This does not mean travel is unimportant; far from it. New places can refresh perception because they strip away familiarity. Suddenly, door handles, breakfast customs, train announcements, birds, money, gestures, and street signs all become vivid. The traveler is forced to notice because autopilot fails. But the deeper challenge is to bring that traveler’s eye home.

Home is where attention is most endangered by familiarity. The more often we see something, the less we see it. The front door, the chipped mug, the view from the window, the faces of those we love—these can become so known that they vanish. Then one day something changes: a move, a death, a renovation, a child leaving home, a tree cut down. Suddenly, the ordinary returns in the form of ache. We realize we had been living among treasures without greeting them.

This is not a call to cling. Everything changes whether noticed or not. The point is not to freeze life in reverence, but to meet it as it passes. To say, while it is still here: this is the kitchen where we danced badly; this is the street where the leaves gather in October; this is the hand I have held for years; this is the sound of my friend’s laugh; this is the body that carries me, imperfectly but faithfully, through the day.

Attention is gratitude’s doorway. Not gratitude as forced optimism or denial of hardship, but gratitude as accurate perception of what is given. It is difficult to be grateful for what one does not notice. The practice begins small: warmth, breath, a clean shirt, a message from someone who remembered, the fact that music exists, the fact that language can carry thought from one solitude to another.

Language itself is one of the miracles we overlook because we use it constantly. Here are marks on a screen, and somehow they become sound in the mind. They summon images, arguments, memories, moods. A word like “cinnamon” can release a smell. A word like “home” can open a wound or a refuge. A sentence written by someone dead for centuries can arrive intact in the present and alter the weather inside a living person. If that is not strange, what is?

Books are among the finest technologies of attention ever invented. They ask us to move at the pace of thought. Unlike many modern streams of information, a book does not rearrange itself to chase our impulses. It waits. It offers a sustained encounter with another mind. Reading is not passive; it is a collaboration between attention and imagination. The page gives signs. The reader builds a world.

Libraries, then, are temples of available attention. They are public storehouses of inwardness. In a library, the living and the dead sit quietly together. A teenager studies for an exam near a retired man reading newspapers, near a child discovering dinosaurs, near someone seeking tax forms, near someone sleeping because it is warm and safe. The library does not ask everyone to want the same thing. It simply makes room for seeking.

Making room may be one of the kindest acts. Attention requires room: mental room, temporal room, emotional room. A culture obsessed with speed leaves little room for noticing. Everything becomes compressed. Meals shrink into fuel. Messages replace conversations. Even leisure becomes optimized. We track, rank, share, and measure experiences while barely experiencing them.

Still, the capacity remains. It waits beneath the noise. Sometimes it returns unexpectedly: during illness, when the body forces slowness; during grief, when the world becomes painfully sharp; during love, when another person’s existence seems endlessly significant; during danger, when every detail matters; during solitude, when the mind, after fidgeting, begins to settle.

We can invite it more gently. We can choose one ordinary thing and give it full attention for a minute: a cup of tea, the sound of traffic, the pattern of our own breathing. We can look at the sky before checking the weather. We can ask someone a question and stay for the answer. We can walk the same route and find one thing we have never noticed before. We can let music play without doing anything else. We can resist the urge to turn every beautiful moment into evidence for others.

There is a freedom in experiencing something without immediately translating it into display. A sunset does not become more real because it is photographed. A meal does not become more delicious because it is documented. A thought does not become more valuable because it is broadcast. Sharing can be joyful, of course, but private attention has its own sanctity. Some moments should be allowed to belong only to themselves.

At the heart of all this is a simple proposition: the world is not finished. No matter how familiar, it remains unfinished in our perception. There is always more to notice than we have noticed. The people we know are not finished. The places we inhabit are not finished. Even we are not finished. Attention keeps reality open.

Maybe that is why indifference feels so deadening. It closes the case too early. It says, “I know what this is,” and stops there. Attention says, “I may not know yet.” It leaves a little door open for complexity, for revision, for awe.

Awe need not be reserved for mountains, cathedrals, eclipses, or the birth of stars. There is awe in the fact that the heart beats without being asked. There is awe in fungus threading through soil, in bees communicating through dance, in the moon pulling oceans, in the brain naming itself, in a seed containing instructions for a tree. There is awe in waking up as the same person and not quite the same person every morning. There is awe in the persistence of kindness despite history’s arguments against it.

To notice these things is not to become naïve. It is to become more fully awake.

And perhaps, at the end, what we want most is not merely to have lived, but to have been here. To have inhabited our days rather than consumed them. To have looked closely enough that the world had a chance to look back. To have loved not generally, but particularly: this face, this voice, this season, this flawed and radiant earth.

The quiet art of noticing will not make us invincible. It will not spare us loss. In fact, it may make loss sharper, because what we notice we may come to cherish. But it also makes life richer before loss arrives. It gives us back the texture of our own existence.

Outside, somewhere, even now, something ordinary is happening. A kettle is beginning to sing. A moth rests against a window. Someone is laughing in another room. A dog lifts its head at a distant sound. Clouds move with their slow, indifferent grace. Light touches the edge of a table and makes it briefly golden.

The world is offering itself again.

The least we can do is notice.

The modern world has become extremely skilled at harvesting attention while weakening the capacity for it. We are surrounded by devices and systems designed to interrupt us, stimulate us, flatter us, alarm us, and return us endlessly to the surface of things. The problem is not technology itself. A phone can connect, inform, guide, translate, preserve, and delight. The problem is the economy built around capturing the mind in fragments. Attention, once treated as a private faculty, has become a commodity. Something wants it all the time.

This makes deliberate attention a quiet form of resistance. To read a long book, to take a walk without headphones, to watch clouds without photographing them, to cook slowly, to mend something instead of replacing it, to sit with grief without immediately converting it into a post or performance—these are not grand rebellions, but they matter. They reclaim the mind from compulsive scattering.

Of course, no one can live in a state of constant radiant awareness. That would be exhausting, maybe unbearable. We need autopilot. We need routine. We need the mercy of not noticing every sensation, every implication, every passing shadow. The goal is not perpetual intensity. It is availability. It is keeping some part of oneself awake enough to be surprised.

Surprise is one of the great pleasures of attention. The world, when actually encountered, keeps exceeding our summaries of it. The stern neighbor feeds stray cats at midnight. The boring meeting contains one sentence that changes your mind. The town you thought ugly has a street of pear trees in bloom. The person you envied is lonely. The person you dismissed is wise. The day you expected to waste gives you a memory you keep for years.

Memory itself seems to depend less on time than on attention. We forget whole weeks of routine and remember three seconds from childhood: the green smell of a garden hose, the metallic taste of fear before a school performance, dust floating in a shaft of afternoon light. Why these moments? Perhaps because, for whatever reason, we were fully there. Attention pressed the experience into us.

This suggests that a longer life is not only a matter of more years, but of more presence within the years given. A day skimmed is shorter than a day inhabited. A conversation half-heard is less life than a conversation received. If we cannot control the length of our lives, perhaps we can influence their depth.

There is an old tension between doing and being. Much advice urges us to act, build, improve, strive. Much wisdom literature urges us to pause, accept, observe, breathe. Both are necessary. A life of pure action can become frantic and hollow. A life of pure contemplation can become passive or detached. Attention may be the bridge between them. It allows action to arise from contact with reality rather than from reflex. It allows stillness to become engaged rather than inert.

Consider gardening. A gardener cannot merely impose an idea upon the soil. They must observe: where the light falls, where water gathers, which leaves yellow, which insects arrive, what the season permits. Gardening is work, certainly, but it is work guided by attention. The same is true of teaching, parenting, carpentry, medicine, friendship, cooking, and almost any meaningful craft. Skill begins where attention deepens.

Even mistakes become more useful when noticed carefully. A cook who burns garlic learns the speed of heat. A musician who plays a wrong note learns the shape of a phrase. A person who speaks harshly and sees another face close in pain learns the consequence of tone. Without attention, mistakes repeat as if they were fate. With attention, they become instruction.

Perhaps wisdom is accumulated attention: not merely having experiences, but having noticed them.

One can travel the world inattentively and remain provincial. One can live in the same town for fifty years attentively and discover endless countries. This does not mean travel is unimportant; far from it. New places can refresh perception because they strip away familiarity. Suddenly, door handles, breakfast customs, train announcements, birds, money, gestures, and street signs all become vivid. The traveler is forced to notice because autopilot fails. But the deeper challenge is to bring that traveler’s eye home.

Home is where attention is most endangered by familiarity. The more often we see something, the less we see it. The front door, the chipped mug, the view from the window, the faces of those we love—these can become so known that they vanish. Then one day something changes: a move, a death, a renovation, a child leaving home, a tree cut down. Suddenly, the ordinary returns in the form of ache. We realize we had been living among treasures without greeting them.

This is not a call to cling. Everything changes whether noticed or not. The point is not to freeze life in reverence, but to meet it as it passes. To say, while it is still here: this is the kitchen where we danced badly; this is the street where the leaves gather in October; this is the hand I have held for years; this is the sound of my friend’s laugh; this is the body that carries me, imperfectly but faithfully, through the day.

Attention is gratitude’s doorway. Not gratitude as forced optimism or denial of hardship, but gratitude as accurate perception of what is given. It is difficult to be grateful for what one does not notice. The practice begins small: warmth, breath, a clean shirt, a message from someone who remembered, the fact that music exists, the fact that language can carry thought from one solitude to another.

Language itself is one of the miracles we overlook because we use it constantly. Here are marks on a screen, and somehow they become sound in the mind. They summon images, arguments, memories, moods. A word like “cinnamon” can release a smell. A word like “home” can open a wound or a refuge. A sentence written by someone dead for centuries can arrive intact in the present and alter the weather inside a living person. If that is not strange, what is?

Books are among the finest technologies of attention ever invented. They ask us to move at the pace of thought. Unlike many modern streams of information, a book does not rearrange itself to chase our impulses. It waits. It offers a sustained encounter with another mind. Reading is not passive; it is a collaboration between attention and imagination. The page gives signs. The reader builds a world.

Libraries, then, are temples of available attention. They are public storehouses of inwardness. In a library, the living and the dead sit quietly together. A teenager studies for an exam near a retired man reading newspapers, near a child discovering dinosaurs, near someone seeking tax forms, near someone sleeping because it is warm and safe. The library does not ask everyone to want the same thing. It simply makes room for seeking.

Making room may be one of the kindest acts. Attention requires room: mental room, temporal room, emotional room. A culture obsessed with speed leaves little room for noticing. Everything becomes compressed. Meals shrink into fuel. Messages replace conversations. Even leisure becomes optimized. We track, rank, share, and measure experiences while barely experiencing them.

Still, the capacity remains. It waits beneath the noise. Sometimes it returns unexpectedly: during illness, when the body forces slowness; during grief, when the world becomes painfully sharp; during love, when another person’s existence seems endlessly significant; during danger, when every detail matters; during solitude, when the mind, after fidgeting, begins to settle.

We can invite it more gently. We can choose one ordinary thing and give it full attention for a minute: a cup of tea, the sound of traffic, the pattern of our own breathing. We can look at the sky before checking the weather. We can ask someone a question and stay for the answer. We can walk the same route and find one thing we have never noticed before. We can let music play without doing anything else. We can resist the urge to turn every beautiful moment into evidence for others.

There is a freedom in experiencing something without immediately translating it into display. A sunset does not become more real because it is photographed. A meal does not become more delicious because it is documented. A thought does not become more valuable because it is broadcast. Sharing can be joyful, of course, but private attention has its own sanctity. Some moments should be allowed to belong only to themselves.

At the heart of all this is a simple proposition: the world is not finished. No matter how familiar, it remains unfinished in our perception. There is always more to notice than we have noticed. The people we know are not finished. The places we inhabit are not finished. Even we are not finished. Attention keeps reality open.

Maybe that is why indifference feels so deadening. It closes the case too early. It says, “I know what this is,” and stops there. Attention says, “I may not know yet.” It leaves a little door open for complexity, for revision, for awe.

Awe need not be reserved for mountains, cathedrals, eclipses, or the birth of stars. There is awe in the fact that the heart beats without being asked. There is awe in fungus threading through soil, in bees communicating through dance, in the moon pulling oceans, in the brain naming itself, in a seed containing instructions for a tree. There is awe in waking up as the same person and not quite the same person every morning. There is awe in the persistence of kindness despite history’s arguments against it.

To notice these things is not to become naïve. It is to become more fully awake.

And perhaps, at the end, what we want most is not merely to have lived, but to have been here. To have inhabited our days rather than consumed them. To have looked closely enough that the world had a chance to look back. To have loved not generally, but particularly: this face, this voice, this season, this flawed and radiant earth.

The quiet art of noticing will not make us invincible. It will not spare us loss. In fact, it may make loss sharper, because what we notice we may come to cherish. But it also makes life richer before loss arrives. It gives us back the texture of our own existence.

Outside, somewhere, even now, something ordinary is happening. A kettle is beginning to sing. A moth rests against a window. Someone is laughing in another room. A dog lifts its head at a distant sound. Clouds move with their slow, indifferent grace. Light touches the edge of a table and makes it briefly golden.

The world is offering itself again.

The least we can do is notice.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
